news_summary: Discoveries of a child's tooth and unusual green stones have shed new light on a 5,500-year-old mystery, advancing our understanding of ancient history.
news_summary_fr: La découverte d'une dent d'enfant et de pierres vertes inhabituelles a apporté un nouvel éclairage sur un mystère vieux de 5 500 ans, faisant ainsi progresser notre compréhension de l'histoire ancienne.
news_summary_es: El hallazgo de un diente de niño y de unas piedras verdes poco comunes ha arrojado nueva luz sobre un misterio de hace 5.500 años, lo que ha permitido avanzar en nuestra comprensión de la historia antigua.

articles.title: A child's tooth and strange green stones uncover a 5,500-year-old mystery
articles.summary: An ancient mountain cave in the Pyrenees may have served as one of the earliest high-altitude mining camps ever discovered, with evidence of repeated visits spanning thousands of years. The find becomes even more intriguing with the discovery of a child’s remains and clues that deeper excavations could uncover prehistoric burials.
articles.content:
High in the eastern Pyrenees, archaeologists have uncovered evidence that could transform our understanding of how prehistoric people used mountain landscapes. A cave perched more than 7,300 feet (2,235 meters) above sea level contains dozens of ancient hearths filled with fragments of green mineral that may point to early copper mining activities.

The discoveries suggest that people repeatedly traveled to this remote location over a span of roughly 2,000 years. The findings challenge the long-standing belief that prehistoric communities only passed briefly through high-altitude environments. Researchers have also recovered a child's finger bone and baby tooth, raising the possibility that the cave may have served as a burial site.

"For a long time, high-mountain environments were seen as marginal, places prehistoric communities passed through occasionally," said Prof Carlos Tornero of the Catalan Institute of Human Paleoecology and Social Evolution, lead author of the article in Frontiers in Environmental Archaeology. "But we found a really rich archaeological sequence, including multiple combustion structures and a very large number of green mineral fragments. We can't say exactly how long people stayed each time, but the repeated use of the space and the density of remains suggest occupations that were short to medium in duration, but happening again and again over long periods of time."

Ancient Hearths and Possible Copper Mining

Known as Cave 338, the site lies in the Freser Valley. Archaeologists excavated a 6-square-meter area near the cave entrance and identified four distinct layers of human activity.

The most recent layer was relatively thin and contained a small number of artifacts from historical periods, suggesting the cave saw limited use during that time. The deepest and oldest layer contained only charcoal fragments, which were dated to approximately 6,000 years ago.

The most significant discoveries came from the second and third layers. Researchers uncovered 23 hearths containing large numbers of crushed and burned fragments of green mineral. Detailed testing is still underway, but the material resembles malachite, a copper-rich mineral that can be processed to produce copper.

If confirmed, the evidence could indicate that Cave 338 functioned as a surprisingly early high-altitude mining camp.

"Many of these fragments are thermally altered, while other materials in the cave are not, which clearly suggests that fire played an important role in their processing and that there was a deliberate intention behind it," said Dr. Julia Montes-Landa of the University of Granada, co-author. "In other words, they weren't burned by accident."

The hearths often overlap one another, showing that the same area was reused repeatedly. At the same time, the individual hearths remain clearly distinct, indicating that the visits were separated by substantial periods of time rather than representing a single continuous occupation.

Radiocarbon dating places the hearth from the second layer at roughly 3,000 years old. The hearths in the third layer date between about 5,500 and 4,000 years ago.

Child's Remains and Prehistoric Jewelry

Researchers also recovered human remains from the third layer, including a finger bone and a baby tooth from at least one child who was about 11 years old. While there is currently not enough evidence to determine a cause of death or whether the two bones belonged to the same child, the discovery raises the possibility that additional burials could be hidden deeper within the cave.

Other artifacts provided clues about the people who visited the site.

"We recovered two pendants: one made from a shell and another from a brown bear tooth," said Tornero. "They come from prehistoric contexts, most likely around the second millennium BC. The shell pendant is interesting because it has parallels in other sites in Catalonia, which suggests shared traditions or connections between different communities. The bear tooth pendant is much less common. That might point to something more specific or symbolic, possibly linked to the local environment."

A Cave Revisited for Thousands of Years

Although Cave 338 was not used as a permanent settlement, the repeated returns over millennia suggest that the location held significant value for prehistoric groups.

Researchers hope future excavations will reveal more about how the cave was used and when people occupied it. They also aim to determine the precise identity of the green mineral and trace its origin.

"The identification of the green mineral as malachite is still preliminary," explained Tornero. "The research ongoing by the University of Granada and the Autonomous University of Barcelona will provide final answers shortly. Also, the excavation hasn't yet reached the full depth of the site, so the sequence is not completely documented. This summer we will continue the archaeological work."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant archaeological discovery that challenges previous assumptions about prehistoric human activity in high-altitude environments, revealing repeated use of a mountain cave for mining and possible burials over thousands of years. This finding has broad implications for understanding human history and prehistoric culture, providing meaningful insight into ancient societies.
articles.personality_title: Ancient mountain cave reveals early mining and child’s remains in the Pyrenees
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – High in the Pyrenees mountains, a cave more than 7,300 feet above sea level has been studied by archaeologists. Until now, experts thought prehistoric people only passed quickly through such high places.

**What happened** – In a cave called Cave 338, researchers found 23 ancient hearths with green mineral fragments that might be early copper ore. They also discovered a child’s finger bone and baby tooth, plus two pendants made from a shell and a bear tooth. These finds come from layers dating back 5,500 to 3,000 years ago.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that prehistoric people repeatedly visited this high mountain cave, possibly for mining copper. It also suggests the cave may have been used for burials. The repeated visits over thousands of years reveal that high-altitude areas were more important to ancient communities than previously thought.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will continue digging deeper this summer to learn more about the cave’s use. They will also test the green mineral to confirm if it is malachite, a copper-rich stone, and try to find where it came from.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Archaeologists uncovered a 5,500-year-old mountain cave used again and again for early copper mining and possibly burials, changing how we see prehistoric life in high places.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une grotte ancienne en montagne révèle des mines précoces et des restes d’enfant dans les Pyrénées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans les Pyrénées, une grotte située à plus de 2 235 mètres d’altitude a été étudiée par des archéologues. Jusqu’à présent, on pensait que les peuples préhistoriques ne passaient que brièvement dans ces zones élevées.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Dans la grotte appelée Grotte 338, les chercheurs ont trouvé 23 foyers anciens avec des fragments d’un minéral vert qui pourrait être du cuivre. Ils ont aussi découvert un os de doigt et une dent de bébé, ainsi que deux pendentifs faits d’une coquille et d’une dent d’ours. Ces objets datent de 5 500 à 3 000 ans.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les peuples préhistoriques revenaient souvent dans cette grotte en montagne, peut-être pour extraire du cuivre. La grotte pourrait aussi avoir servi de lieu d’inhumation. Ces visites répétées révèlent que les zones en haute altitude étaient plus importantes qu’on le pensait.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer les fouilles cet été pour mieux comprendre l’usage de la grotte. Ils vont aussi analyser le minéral vert pour confirmer s’il s’agit de malachite, un minerai de cuivre, et chercher son origine.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des archéologues ont découvert une grotte en montagne vieille de 5 500 ans, utilisée régulièrement pour l’extraction précoce du cuivre et peut-être pour des enterrements, changeant notre vision de la vie préhistorique en altitude.
articles.personality_title_es: Una cueva antigua en la montaña revela minería temprana y restos de un niño en los Pirineos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En los Pirineos, una cueva situada a más de 2,235 metros sobre el nivel del mar fue estudiada por arqueólogos. Hasta ahora, se creía que los pueblos prehistóricos solo pasaban brevemente por estas zonas altas.

**Qué pasó** – En la cueva llamada Cueva 338, los investigadores encontraron 23 hogueras antiguas con fragmentos de un mineral verde que podría ser cobre. También descubrieron un hueso de dedo y un diente de bebé, junto con dos colgantes hechos de una concha y un diente de oso. Estas piezas datan de entre 5,500 y 3,000 años atrás.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que los pueblos prehistóricos visitaban repetidamente esta cueva en la montaña, posiblemente para extraer cobre. También sugiere que la cueva pudo usarse para entierros. Las visitas repetidas durante miles de años muestran que las zonas de gran altitud eran más importantes de lo que se pensaba.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continuarán excavando más profundamente este verano para aprender más sobre el uso de la cueva. También analizarán el mineral verde para confirmar si es malaquita, un mineral rico en cobre, y buscarán su origen.

**Resumen en una frase** – Arqueólogos descubrieron una cueva de 5,500 años en la montaña usada repetidamente para minería temprana de cobre y posiblemente entierros, cambiando la visión sobre la vida prehistórica en lugares altos.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing ancient cave entrance high in the Pyrenees mountains, with layered stone hearths containing glowing green malachite fragments and faint ghostly silhouettes of a child’s finger bone and baby tooth subtly embedded in the rocky walls, surrounded by symbolic pendants shaped like a shell and a bear tooth resting gently nearby, all rendered in warm earthy tones with delicate brushstrokes conveying timeless reverence and repeated human presence.
